extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Digital Twin targets a thermal incubator controlled by a Raspberry Pi.  The hardware stack consists of an insulated styrofoam box (providing thermal isolation), a heat‑bed (electric heating element), a fan (to turbulently mix the air and enforce spatial temperature uniformity), two internal temperature sensors (in‑box), and one external temperature sensor.  The controller is a simple bang‑bang (on‑off) logic that turns the heat‑bed on until the measured temperature reaches an upper limit, then turns it off until it falls below a lower limit, with configurable heating and waiting durations.  The system operates in a laboratory/biological environment where maintaining a precise temperature set point (e.g., for embryonic development) is critical.  The incubator’s passive thermal inertia (heat‑bed, air, enclosure) and the need for rapid, accurate temperature predictions make it an ideal candidate for Digital Twin exploration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Through a message‑bus‑based Digital Twin framework the system offers real‑time monitoring, calibration, and predictive services. Sensors publish temperature readings to a RabbitMQ server, which is consumed by DT components that (1) continuously track PT status and log data; (2) run model‑calibration routines that adjust the parameters of Model B to match observed thermal dynamics; (3) perform anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman‑filter that estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; (4) execute what‑if simulations via co‑simulation that inject historical or live PT data to evaluate controller performance; and (5) support a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detecting model drift, schedules experiments, recalibrates, reconfigures the controller, and re‑optimizes control parameters. These services serve operators and engineering teams responsible for maintaining safe and optimal temperature control.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built on a combination of open‑source hardware and software: a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B runs the low‑level driver and bang‑bang controller, communicating with the physical sensors and actuators through a safe protocol. Messages are exchanged via a RabbitMQ server hosted locally; the DT components listen to the message queue to ingest sensor data and publish control commands. Model development and calibration scripts are maintained in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) and rely on the open‑source ecosystem for simulation and model calibration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Digital Twin architecture consists of a controller running on a Raspberry Pi, a low-level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator communication, and a RabbitMQ communication server that exchanges AMQP messages between the controller and the driver. The Twin updates its embedded dynamic models (Model A and Model B) through a calibration algorithm that uses sensor data, and it runs monitoring functions that check safety and performance. All components are integrated within the same process frame and communicate over the RabbitMQ broker, allowing the model, simulation, and monitoring services to operate synchronously with the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The DT integrates externally with the RabbitMQ message broker using the AMQP protocol to exchange sensor readings, control commands, and telemetry. No additional external enterprise systems, standards, or other Digital Twins are referenced in the documents. The open‑source code repository hosts all software but is not an external integration point for runtime data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model A and Model B are the computational simulation models used to predict the interior temperature of the incubator. Model A incorporates all four modelling assumptions (uniform temperature, negligible wall heat, constant specific heat of air, and that all electrical power is converted into heating the air) and contains two free parameters to be identified. Model B relaxes assumption 4, accounting for the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed, and therefore contains four free parameters. Both models derive from the energy balance Q = c m ΔT and the power input equation E = V I Δt. The data component comprises temperature measurements from two internal thermocouples in the styrofoam box and one external thermocouple measuring ambient temperature. The data are read periodically by the low‑level driver, transmitted via the RabbitMQ communication server to the controller, and subsequently to the Digital Twin. A least‑squares calibration algorithm updates the model parameters using the collected data. The Digital Twin framework integrates these models, the measured data, and calibration algorithms, following the generic DT architecture of data + models + algorithms outlined in Document 1.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators are the heat‑bed and the installed fan. The heat‑bed is an electrical heating pad whose power is supplied by the Raspberry Pi controller; it can be switched on or off via a command received from the controller. The fan is an electric motor that distributes the heated air within the styrofoam enclosure; its operation state is also controlled by the controller. The controller implements a bang‑bang control logic with the parameters LL (lower limit), UL (upper limit), H (heating duration), and C (waiting duration). Commands are sent to the hardware through the low‑level driver, which operates as a safety‑aware interface: it verifies that any received command is within permissible bounds before switching the actuators. Communication with the controller and the low‑level driver relies on RabbitMQ message broker, providing the remote control interface. Actuator ranges are limited to on/off states; safety constraints require the device to enter a safe state when no command is received or when an unexpected condition is detected.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors are installed: two inside the styrofoam enclosure to monitor the internal temperature and one external sensor to measure ambient temperature. Sensors are connected to the Raspberry Pi via the low‑level driver; data are read periodically, timestamped, and forwarded to RabbitMQ as lightweight JSON messages. The fan inside the box ensures uniform temperature distribution, satisfying the assumption that the enclosure’s temperature can be treated as spatially uniform. Each sensor’s reading is transmitted to the data‑base, allowing real‑time visualization and calibration.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is governed by four assumptions: 1) the temperature inside the box is spatially uniform due to the fan; 2) thermal accumulation in the box walls is negligible; 3) the specific heat capacity of air is constant despite pressure or humidity changes; 4) nearly all electrical power supplied to the heat‑bed is converted to heating the air (the heat generated by the fan is neglected). Model A applies all four assumptions and contains two free parameters; Model B omits assumption 4 and has four free parameters, enabling better prediction of the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia. Validation is performed by calibrating the model parameters using a least‑squares fit against the measured temperature data from the sensors. Calibration updates the parameter estimates to produce the best‑fit model at each iteration. The models are then compared against measured data to assess residual errors. Although explicit error‑correction mechanisms are not described, the calibration algorithm ensures that model predictions remain aligned with physical measurements, thereby maintaining both fidelity and validity appropriate for the Digital Twin application.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the two inside temperature sensors (in °C) and one ambient sensor are read periodically by the low‑level driver, then published as messages to the RabbitMQ communication server. Messages use the AMQP protocol; payload consists of raw temperature readings (numeric values). No explicit preprocessing or data quality filtering is described in the documents. The DT components (e.g., controller, monitoring modules) consume these messages directly from RabbitMQ. Transmission is dependent on the low‑level driver’s polling interval, which is unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DT controller calculates the control action (turn fan/heatbed on or off) based on the latest temperature telemetry. It publishes the command to the local RabbitMQ queue. The low‑level driver receives the command, validates it against a safety protocol that prevents unsafe operations (e.g., disabling the heatbed if the temperature exceeds the upper limit), and transmits the actuator instruction to the physical twin. The driver confirms execution by publishing the actuator state back to RabbitMQ, allowing the DT to maintain an up‑to‑date view of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All intra‑system communication is mediated by a local RabbitMQ message broker instantiated on the Raspberry Pi that serves the incubator. The broker exchanges messages between the low‑level driver, the DT controller, and any external monitoring services. RabbitMQ provides the messaging middleware that enables asynchronous, decoupled interaction among these components. No additional network topology or specialized protocols beyond this broker are defined in the case study.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire DT stack runs on‑premise on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, which hosts the Raspberry Pi OS (Debian‑based), RabbitMQ, the low‑level driver, and the controller. The deployment is based on open‑source containers/packages available through the repository https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator. The setup requires only a modest compute node; no cloud or external hosting is discussed. Scalability is limited to the Pi’s resources; for larger scale deployments, the architecture would need to be extended.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The documents do not provide a formal description of Digital Twin lifecycle stages, management of representation types, transition processes, or governance approaches.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT engineering process involves: • data acquisition from the physical incubator via Raspberry Pi sensors, read by a low‑level driver; • data and command exchange through RabbitMQ message broker; • model development: two thermal models (Model A and the more accurate Model B) derived from energy balance equations (Q = cm ΔT) and calibrated by optimizing parameters (V I Δt) to fit measured temperature trajectories; • calibration is performed on demand via the procedure described in section 3.3, producing updated parameters for Model B; • simulation of the physical twin is enabled by running these models in co‑simulation with real‑time or historical sensor streams; • the Digital Twin incorporates a Kalman filter that fuses model predictions with measurements to estimate hidden states (e.g., heat‑bed temperature); • a bang‑bang controller on the Raspberry Pi uses temperature thresholds (LL, UL, H, C) to issue heat‑bed and fan commands, and status messages are published back to RabbitMQ; • no explicit version control, quality assurance, or testing procedures are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides decision support by: • real‑time visualization of incubator state through a time‑series database and dashboard that consumes controller and driver messages; • anomaly detection and state estimation via a Kalman filter using Model B, which correlates measured temperatures with unmeasured heat‑bed temperature, producing residuals used to flag deviations; • what‑if scenario analysis by running co‑simulations that feed historic or live PT data into the models to predict the effects of controller parameter changes; • a self‑adaptation routine that detects plant behaviour changes (via filter residuals), schedules experiments, recalibrates Model B, re‑configures the filter and controller, and re‑optimises controller parameters; • user interaction is primarily through the dashboard interface, without explicit confidence metrics reported.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Incubator DT uses RabbitMQ as the communication broker to transmit sensor readings and command messages between the Raspberry‑Pi controller, the low‑level driver, and external data services.  The low‑level driver incorporates a built‑in safety protocol that periodically polls the PT temperature sensors, validates incoming control commands, and ensures actuator states (heatbed and fan) are only enabled within defined safe bounds.  The controller implements a bang‑bang logic with configurable lower (LL) and upper (UL) temperature limits, heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C) to prevent temperature overshoot and to allow thermal stabilization.  These mechanisms provide run‑time fail‑safe behaviour and mitigate risks of unsafe temperature excursions.  The documents do not describe additional cybersecurity mechanisms such as encryption, authentication, or access control, nor do they specify compliance with data protection regulations or privacy‑preserving data handling policies.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
